ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for tuna-filled bread roll; closest defensible match is 'bread roll food product' (FOODON_03315595). Tuna filling is not captured in this term.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A00LC covers bread rolls and buns as a category; no specific filled-bun code exists. Tuna-mayo filling is not represented in the FoodEx2 hierarchy.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for tuna bun as a composite food product.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard tuna bun weighs approximately 100 g and constitutes a typical single-serve portion as sold at Singapore bakeries and convenience stores.

nutrient_source.title: Tuna Bun — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Tuna Bun (cr_id F05020115). All core macros and secondary nutrients are taken from this canonical entry with confidence 0.95.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — white bread analogous estimate
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. Diabetes Care 2008;31(12):2281-2283.
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for a tuna-filled white bread bun. GI of 71 is estimated from white bread (GI 71, University of Sydney GI Database), which is the dominant carbohydrate matrix of this product. The tuna-mayonnaise filling contributes negligible carbohydrate and does not materially alter the GI of the bread component. Glycemic load calculated as (GI × available carbohydrate per serving) / 100 = (71 × 33.45) / 100 ≈ 23.7 per 100 g serving.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 422 mg/100 g exceeds the 400 mg/100 g threshold; low dietary fibre (2.0 g/100 g) and absent potassium data provide insufficient offset.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit tuna bun consumption to occasional use, choose low-sodium canned tuna and reduced-fat mayonnaise, and avoid pairing with other high-sodium foods to keep total meal sodium below 600 mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High-GI white bun (GI ≈ 71) with 33.45 g carbohydrate and 8.45 g sugar per 100 g produces a glycemic load of approximately 23.7, well above the low-GL threshold of 10.
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should limit or avoid the standard white-bun version; substituting a wholemeal bun lowers GI and increases fibre, and portion should be restricted to half a bun to reduce postprandial glucose excursion.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Estimated trans fat (0.1 g/100 g from mayonnaise and processed bun) and elevated sodium (422 mg/100 g) are adverse cardiovascular factors; tuna provides cardioprotective omega-3 fatty acids that partially offset risk.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: For cardiovascular health, choose trans-fat-free mayonnaise, low-sodium canned tuna, and a wholemeal bun to reduce atherogenic and hypertensive load while retaining the omega-3 benefit of the tuna filling.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Estimated trans fat from mayonnaise and processed bun is a pro-inflammatory hard disqualifier for RA; refined white flour is a high-GI carbohydrate that drives systemic inflammation.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid the standard tuna bun; switching to trans-fat-free mayonnaise and a wholemeal bun reduces inflammatory load, while retaining the anti-inflammatory omega-3 benefit of the tuna filling.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Tuna bun is a popular Singaporean bakery and convenience-store snack consisting of a soft white bread roll filled with a tuna-mayonnaise mixture. The bread component is a lightly sweetened, enriched white dough bun, baked until golden and yielding a pillowy, slightly chewy crumb. The filling typically combines canned tuna (in brine or oil, drained) with full-fat or reduced-fat mayonnaise, sometimes seasoned with black pepper, onion, or celery. The result is a moist, savoury filling encased in a soft, mildly sweet bun. Per 100 g, the tuna bun delivers 302 kcal, 12.25 g protein, 13.3 g fat, and 33.45 g carbohydrate, with 8.45 g sugar, 2.0 g dietary fibre, and 422 mg sodium. The white bun matrix carries a high glycemic index (approximately 71), producing a glycemic load of approximately 23.7 per 100 g, making it a high-GL food unsuitable for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes or insulin resistance without portion control. Sodium at 422 mg/100 g exceeds the 400 mg threshold relevant to hypertension management. Estimated trans fat from mayonnaise and processed bun ingredients is a concern for rheumatoid arthritis and cardiovascular disease. The tuna filling contributes omega-3 fatty acids and high-quality protein, which are cardioprotective and anti-inflammatory. The tuna bun is widely consumed as a breakfast item, school snack, or light lunch across Singapore, sold at bakeries, hawker centres, and convenience stores. Halal-certified versions are widely available.
common_user_queries: How many calories in a tuna bun?
Is tuna bun suitable for diabetics?
Can I eat tuna bun if I have high blood pressure?

confidence_reason: Core nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id F05020115) with confidence 0.95. GI is estimated from the white bread analogue in the University of Sydney GI Database, which is the standard defensible approach for composite bread-based products without a direct GI measurement. Saturated fat and trans fat are estimated from standard composition of mayonnaise and white bread components using USDA FoodData Central reference values, documented in notes.
notes: 1. Saturated fat (2.1 g/100 g) and trans fat (0.1 g/100 g) are not reported in the HPB source entry; these values are estimated from USDA FoodData Central reference values for white bread (USDA FDC ID 325871) and commercial mayonnaise (USDA FDC ID 172741) weighted by approximate ingredient proportions (60% bun, 30% tuna, 10% mayonnaise). These are approximate and should be replaced with direct analytical values when available. 2. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, potassium_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractions, micronutrient panel, and moisture data for this composite tuna bun product are not available in the HPB Foodo database or USDA FoodData Central as a matched composite entry as of 2026-04-16. 3. GI of 71 is an analogous estimate based on white bread (GI 71, University of Sydney GI Database); no direct GI measurement for a tuna-filled white bread bun has been published in peer-reviewed literature as of 2026-04-16. 4. Halal status is marked true based on widespread availability of halal-certified tuna buns in Singapore; individual product halal certification should be verified. 5. Trans fat 0.1 g/100 g is an estimate; actual trans fat content depends on the specific mayonnaise brand used.
